title: The part that creates emergencies to avoid sitting with emptiness
author: Kalesh
excerpt: I know this part of you. The one that scans the horizon for something, anything, to react to. The part that creates emergencies when everything is actually qui...
metaDescription: I know this part of you. The one that scans the horizon for something, anything, to react to. The part that creates emergencies when everything is actuall...
htmlContent:
I know this part of you. The one that scans the horizon for something, anything, to react to. The part that creates emergencies when everything is actually quiet. The part that picks a fight with a partner over nothing, or suddenly remembers an urgent work task at 10 PM, or convinces you that a minor physical sensation is definitely a medical crisis. I know this part because I have it too. It lives in my chest like a coiled spring, always ready to snap into action the moment the world goes still.

Here's the thing. That part isn't broken. It's not trying to ruin your life. It's trying to save you from something far more terrifying than any external emergency. It's trying to save you from the emptiness inside.

Stay with me here. I want to talk about what that emptiness actually is, and why your system treats it like a five-alarm fire.

The emptiness that terrifies your nervous system

When I say emptiness, I don't mean the kind you feel on a lazy Sunday afternoon when you don't have plans. I mean the kind that lives in your bones. The kind that comes from early experiences where your needs weren't met, where your attachment figures weren't reliably there, where you learned that being alone meant being unsafe. That emptiness isn't just boredom. It's a direct line back to the original wound of being left, being unseen, being unheld.

Your nervous system knows this. It remembers. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades watching how trauma gets stored in the body. He showed that the body doesn't forget. It holds the memory of every moment you were left to fend for yourself, every time you cried out and no one came, every time you needed comfort and got silence. That memory lives in your tissues, in your vagus nerve, in the way your breath gets shallow when you're alone.

So when life gets quiet, when the distractions fall away, when there's no crisis to manage and no fire to put out, your system doesn't feel peaceful. It feels threatened. Because silence was never safe. Quiet was where the pain lived. And your protective part knows that if you sit still long enough, you might actually feel it.

That's where the emergency-maker comes in. It's a firefighter with a hose full of gasoline, but it means well. It creates a crisis because crisis is familiar. Crisis is manageable. Crisis gives you something to do, something to fix, something to control. Emptiness gives you nothing. And nothing, for a traumatized nervous system, is the scariest thing there is.

Think about it. When was the last time you had a completely uneventful day and didn't feel a low-grade sense of dread? When was the last time you sat in silence without reaching for your phone, your to-do list, or a conflict to resolve? If that question makes your stomach clench, you know exactly what I'm talking about.

How the emergency-maker operates in daily life

This part doesn't always announce itself with obvious drama. Sometimes it's subtle. It's the way you suddenly remember that email you haven't sent, and it becomes an urgent priority at 11 PM. It's the way you pick at a small disagreement until it becomes a full-blown fight. It's the way you convince yourself that a headache is a brain tumor, or that your partner's distracted mood means they're going to leave you.

I once had a client who would clean her entire house at 2 AM whenever she had a quiet weekend ahead. She wasn't a neat freak. She was trying to outrun the silence. The cleaning gave her a mission, a purpose, a way to stay in her head instead of dropping into her body. She told me she felt "crazy" when she did it. I told her she was just trying to survive.

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, calls these parts "firefighters." They're the parts that rush in when something painful threatens to surface. They don't care about long-term consequences. They just want to put out the emotional fire, and they'll use anything to do it: work, conflict, substances, scrolling, eating, not eating, exercise, illness, catastrophizing. Whatever works fastest.

The emergency-maker is a firefighter part. And like all firefighters, it's trying to protect you from something it believes will destroy you. In this case, that something is the emptiness. The part believes that if you stop running, if you stop creating emergencies, you'll fall into a black hole of despair that you'll never climb out of. And it's not entirely wrong. The feelings that live in that emptiness are real, and they're old, and they hurt. But they won't kill you. The part doesn't know that yet.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work directly addresses these protective parts and how they keep us stuck in survival mode. I've recommended it to more people than I can count, and it's helped many start to see their emergency-maker with compassion instead of shame.

The cost of constant emergency

Here's the thing about living in a state of perpetual crisis. It works, until it doesn't. Your body was not designed to run at full alert forever. The nervous system has a cost for all that emergency activation, and it's going to collect eventually.

You might notice it in your sleep, or lack thereof. You might notice it in your digestion, your skin, your immune system. You might notice it in the relationships that have frayed because you're always in reaction mode, always on edge, always looking for the next threat. The emergency-maker is exhausting to live with. Not just for you, but for everyone around you.

And here's the cruel irony. The very crises you create to avoid the emptiness actually deepen the emptiness. Because every time you choose the emergency, you're telling your nervous system that you can't handle stillness. You're reinforcing the belief that being alone with yourself is dangerous. You're training your system to need chaos to feel safe. It's a trap, and it's a good one.

I know because I've been in it. For years, I filled every quiet moment with something. A project, a problem, a drama, a worry. I was never just sitting. I was always doing, always planning, always preparing for disaster. And the disaster never came, but neither did any real peace. I was too busy fighting fires I had set myself.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) by Janina Fisher. She explains how these parts develop and how they can learn to trust that you're safe now, even when the external world is calm. It's not an easy read, but it's a necessary one if you want to understand the architecture of your own mind.

What happens when you stop running

I'm not going to tell you that sitting with emptiness is easy. It's not. The first time you try to just be still, without creating a crisis, your nervous system will scream at you. It will throw up every distraction, every worry, every urgent thought it can find. Your emergency-maker will try to convince you that you're wasting time, that you're being irresponsible, that something terrible will happen if you don't act now.

That's okay. That's normal. That's the part doing its job. The goal isn't to get rid of it. The goal is to let it know you've got this. To tell it, gently, that you can handle the emptiness now. That you're an adult with resources, and you don't need to be saved from your own feelings.

Start small. Five minutes. Sit in a chair, no phone, no TV, no task. Just you and the silence. Notice what comes up. Maybe it's restlessness. Maybe it's sadness. Maybe it's a memory you haven't thought about in years. Let it be there. Don't fix it. Don't fight it. Don't turn it into an emergency. Just breathe.

Your part will panic at first. It will throw everything it has at you. But if you stay, if you keep breathing, something shifts. The panic settles. The emptiness becomes less terrifying. You realize it's not a void. It's a space. And in that space, there's room for something new. Curiosity. Grief. Tenderness. Maybe even peace.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It's a warm, accessible book that helped me see my emergency-maker not as an enemy, but as a scared protector trying to do its job. It changed how I talk to that part of myself.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Because sometimes the emergency-maker is also trying to protect boundaries that were never respected. Learning to set clear, calm limits can reduce the need for dramatic crisis creation.

The part that isn't an emergency

I want to be honest with you. I still have this part. It still shows up, especially when I'm tired or stressed or when something reminds me of old pain. The difference is that I know it now. I see it coming. And instead of following it blindly into the next crisis, I can pause.

I can say, "I see you. I know you're scared. I know you think we need a fire right now. But we don't. We're okay. Let's just sit with this feeling for a minute."

Sometimes the part listens. Sometimes it doesn't. Sometimes I still create the emergency. I'm human. But more and more, I choose to sit with the emptiness. And I've found that it's not empty at all. It's full of things I was too afraid to feel. Sadness. Longing. Gratitude. Aliveness. The stuff of being a person.

Your emergency-maker is not your enemy. It's your protector. It's the part that kept you alive when stillness meant danger. But you're not in that danger anymore. You're here, now, in a body that can learn to rest. And the emptiness you've been running from? It might just be the place where you finally meet yourself.

No neat ending here. Just an invitation. Next time you feel the urge to create a crisis, pause. Take a breath. Ask the part what it's afraid will happen if you just sit still. And then, if you can, sit still anyway. See what's there. It might surprise you.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - For understanding how trauma fragments the self
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
